topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp autumn air did little to settle the knot tightening in Eleanor’s chest. She was sorting through old school records, a task she’d set herself to clear out the attic, when she found it—a faded photograph tucked inside a yearbook. In the picture, a smiling, younger version of her daughter stood beside a woman with kind eyes and a cascade of silver hair. Eleanor’s breath hitched. The woman in the photograph was Mrs. Albright, her daughter’s kindergarten teacher. A cold, hollow feeling spread through Eleanor’s limbs, making her hands tremble as she carefully folded the picture back into its sleeve. She walked to the window, staring out at the familiar street, feeling as though the very foundations of her known world had just shifted, leaving her unsteady on her feet.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this, Dad?" Sarah asked, her voice light, but Eleanor felt a sudden, sharp clench in her stomach. She held up the enrollment forms, the paper feeling suddenly brittle beneath her fingertips. The name printed clearly at the top was Mrs. Albright. It was impossible. Her daughter, Maya, had been in Mrs. Albright’s class for two years. A wave of nausea washed over Eleanor, forcing her to grip the edge of the desk until her knuckles were white. She looked at Sarah, trying to keep her expression neutral, but her gaze kept flicking back to the name, a strange, unsettling echo ringing in the silence of the room. Every mundane object in the living room seemed suddenly charged with unspoken significance.

[story 3]

Mark traced the outline of the photo with a fingertip, the edges soft and worn. He hadn't meant to find it; it had simply materialized in his hand while rummaging through boxes of forgotten school supplies. When he saw the familiar face of Mrs. Albright, the easy calm he usually maintained fractured. His jaw tightened, and he stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the study. "This can't be right," he murmured, the words barely audible. A profound sense of dislocation settled over him, heavy and suffocating, making him want to simply retreat into the shadows, away from the sudden, unwelcome clarity of the situation.
